coredata.dc:title: Hydrothermal route upcycling surgical masks into dual-emitting carbon dots as ratiometric fluorescent probe for Cr (VI) and corrosion inhibitor in saline solution
coredata.dc:creator: Miao, Caiqin
Wang, Qun
Yang, Shuang
Tang, Yihui
Liu, Xiyan
Lu, Songtao
coredata.dc:description: Exploration effective route to convert plastic waste into valuable carbon dots with bifunction of metal fluorescence monitoring and corrosion protection in seawater is promising. Herein, using “white-pollution” polypropylene surgical masks as a single precursor, dual-emitting carbon dots (CDs) with excellent ratiometric fluorescent sensitivity and corrosion inhibitor efficiency were fabricated with high yield (∼100 %) by a one-pot in situ acid oxidation hydrothermal strategy without post-treatment and organic solvents. Chemical, structural, morphological, optical properties and the Cr (VI) detection and Cu inhibition mechanism of the synthesized CDs had been systematically studied. Furthermore, a dual-response-OFF proportional fluorescent probe had been developed for the detection of the analyte Cr (VI) with a low detection limit of 24 nM. Additionally, the corrosion inhibition efficiency of the prepared CDs reached approximately 94.01 % for Cu substrate in 3.5 wt% NaCl electrolyte under a CDs concentration of 200 mg/L, which is higher than that of most previous reports.

(b) The normalized UV–vis absorption spectra (black line) and normalized fluorescence (FL) excitation (blue line) and emission (red line) spectra of the CDs in water (The optimum excitation and emission wavelength of CDs are 340 nm and 394, 486 nm, respectively). (c) CIE color coordinates of the CDs. (d) The fluorescence stability of the CDs for 30 days.

head.ce:title: Hydrothermal route upcycling surgical masks into dual-emitting carbon dots as ratiometric fluorescent probe for Cr (VI) and corrosion inhibitor in saline solution

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Harbin Institute of Technology, Harbin, 150001, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Harbin Institute of Technology, Harbin, 150001, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Exploration effective route to convert plastic waste into valuable carbon dots with bifunction of metal fluorescence monitoring and corrosion protection in seawater is promising. Herein, using “white-pollution” polypropylene surgical masks as a single precursor, dual-emitting carbon dots (CDs) with excellent ratiometric fluorescent sensitivity and corrosion inhibitor efficiency were fabricated with high yield (∼100 %) by a one-pot in situ acid oxidation hydrothermal strategy without post-treatment and organic solvents. Chemical, structural, morphological, optical properties and the Cr (VI) detection and Cu inhibition mechanism of the synthesized CDs had been systematically studied. Furthermore, a dual-response-OFF proportional fluorescent probe had been developed for the detection of the analyte Cr (VI) with a low detection limit of 24 nM. Additionally, the corrosion inhibition efficiency of the prepared CDs reached approximately 94.01 % for Cu substrate in 3.5 wt% NaCl electrolyte under a CDs concentration of 200 mg/L, which is higher than that of most previous reports.

“White-pollution” disposable polystyrene surgical masks were converted to carbon dots (CDs) with surface carboxylate oxygen-containing groups which exhibited good performance as a novel dual-response-off type ratiometric probe and corrosion inhibitors for copper in 3.5 wt% NaCl medium.
Image 1

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Plastic waste has become a global problem because of exceedingly difficult for natural degradation. It is estimated that more than 500 million metric tonnes of plastics will be required in 2050 and roughly 79 % are discarded in the land and ocean. The convenient burning solution causes air pollution and climate change. Various violent and mild strategies such as photocatalysis and biocatalysis etc. were carried out to degrade plastic wastes [1,2]. Specially, surgical face masks made of polypropylene (PP) polymer [3,4] are difficult to recycle and biodegradable, which are indispensable in the healthcare sector and public health safety during in COVID-19 pandemic. To economically and environmentally dispose of accumulated plastic face masks, researchers recently investigated numerous methods of converting them into high-value kinds of carbon materials [5–8]. For instance, polypropylene (PP) was deconstructed by catalyst into graphitic nanofibers for Na ion battery [5]. Discarded plastic masks-derived 3D porous carbon [6] and 1D carbon-nickel composite nanowires [7,8] were used for lithium-based batteries and superior microwave absorption, respectively. Beside 3D and 1D materials, as a promising zero-dimesional carbon-based material, carbon dots (CDs) had gained tremendous research attention worldwide owing to their high photoluminescence intensity, biocompatibility, chemical stability and low toxicity [9,10]. However, there are few reports about the development of CDs derived from surgical plastic face masks without catalyst and organic solvent.

Recently, the carbon nanomaterials with CC covalent bond exhibited superior extreme environmental applications [11]. Specially, except for deep space of universe, the ocean represents a type of extreme environment as seawater with high salinity and high Cl− concentration, which easily triggers pit corrosion on the metal surface and causes degradation of their mechanical strength and greatly shortens their service life [11]. Copper has been extensively employed in multitudes of fields including ocean-going ships and nuclear power industry. Conventional inorganic corrosion inhibitors contain poisonous phosphate, chromate, etc., thus damaging human health and polluting the sea environment. Recently, the functionalized carbon dots (CDs) as an eco-friendly corrosion inhibitor for copper in sulfuric acid [12,13] were developed. Nevertheless, they still cannot be considered as ideal green corrosion inhibitors because they suffered from tedious preparation procedures for high-cost raw precursor such as amino acid [12], biomass [13] and glucose [14]. Moreover, they possessed an unsatisfactory inhibition efficiency in neutral high salinity solutions.

On the other hand, chromium (Cr) and its alloy coatings are widely served as the corrosion-resistant metals in seawater surrounding and in various industrial processes, such as textiles, dyes, electroplating, stainless steel, leather and so on [15]. Soluble Cr(VI) in the form of thermodynamically stable chromate (CrO4 2−) in the ocean plays an important role in marine ecosystems [16,17]. At present, the reported typical methods for Cr (VI) detection including inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS), ultraviolet–visible spectrophotometry (UV), ion chromatography (IC), and atomic absorption spectrometry inevitably suffer from the drawbacks of expensive cost and cumbersome testing process. On the contrast, fluorescence method has been developed for the detection of Cr due to its low cost, time-saving and the absence of sophisticated instruments [18,19]. However, they mainly focused on the quenching or enhancement of single emission intensity called “single-response-OFF” [20,21]. In comparison, CDs-based ratiometric fluorescence (FL) probes which only relies on the two emissions of dual-emitting CDs without the introduction of additional luminescent units have dual-response types in order to eliminate the background interference and provide more accurate detection results than single emission intensity-based nanoprobes [22]. For instance, the o-phenylenediamine derived CDs is dual-response-reverse type for the Cr detection that two peaks with intensity ratio I565/I445 are linearly changed [23]. However, CDs-based ratiometric FL probes with dual-response-OFF type for Cr ion detection remains not fully uncovered.

Herein, we synthesized carbon dots (CDs) with dual emission and ∼100 % production yield from polypropylene surgical masks using a facile, single-step, catalyst-free and organic solvent-free hydrothermal approach. Moreover, the Cr ion detection by dual-response-OFF type and corrosion behavior of CDs for Cu in salt water had been systematically investigated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication and characterization of dual-emitting CDs
ce:section.ce:para: It is well known that surgical face masks are composed of waterproof layer, filter layer, ear strap and nose strip, which are bonded together by hot pressing. In our experiments, CDs were prepared from polypropylene-based filter layer through low-temperature, one-step oxidative hydrothermal procedures using a diluted nitric acid solution with the concentration of 0.15 g•mL−1. By tuning the temperatures (140, 160, 180, and 200 °C) for 12 h to determine the optimal temperature for oxidative degradation. The sample was characterized by powder X-ray diffractometer in the 2θ range of 10–60° and Fourier transform infrared spectra (FTIR) was obtained using a Thermo Nicolet iS50 spectrometer. TEM and HRTEM were measured with an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) test was performed to obtain the chemical state of the sample using the ThermoFisher ESCALAB 250Xi spectrometer.

To assess the selectivity of CDs for Cr (VI), 50 μL of CDs (2.5 mg/L) dispersion was added to 50 μL of sensing solution containing 10 mM of Cr (VI) and 10 mM of various other interfering ions (CH2COO)2Hg, Na2SO4, CuSO4, NH4Cl, Se2Cl2, BiCl3, CrCl3 and K2CrO4). Moreover, fluorescence titration experiments were performed in different concentrations of Cr (VI) (0–900 μM at intervals of 100) and the linear relationship between I394/I486 and Cr (VI) concentration was derived, which in turn led to the slope k, and finally the lowest detection limit was calculated by 3σ/k. The above experiments were conducted at the ideal excitation wavelength of 340 nm and each group was collected three times in parallel to minimize the experimental errors.

The copper sheets are polished with 400–2000 grit sandpaper, then ultrasonically cleaned with ultra-pure water and ethanol, and eventually dried naturally. 1 cm2 one side of copper electrode encapsulated with epoxy resin was exposed to the electrolyte including different concentrations of CDs (25–200 mg/L) with 3.5 wt% NaCl solution for electrochemical testing. The three-electrode system of the CHI660E electrochemical workstation was subjected to open circuit potential (OCP), potentiodynamic polarization (PDP) at a scan rate of 2 mV•s−1 and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) in the 105-10−2 frequency range under stable conditions. Furthermore, the acquired EIS data were fitted using ZView software to capture information on relevant corrosion parameters.

Fig. 1 a illustrated the fabrication process of the CDs through a one-pot in situ acid oxidation hydrothermal strategy under autogenous high pressure without post-treatment and organic solvents using “white-pollution” polypropylene surgical masks as a single precursor at 180 °C for 12 h. Fig. 1b exhibited the fluorescence spectra of the prepared CDs at different reaction temperature. It is clearly seen that the weak fluorescence intensity could be observed at 140 and 160 °C, while highest fluorescence intensity at 180 °C appeared and then it decreased at 200 °C, which is similar to the optimal temperature of 3-Aminophenylboronic acid derived CDs (160 °C) [18]. It is indicated that the hydrothermal temperature and autogenous pressure is vital to the molecule and nano/microstructure formation of the CDs. Of course, it is believed that the optical properties of CDs can also be altered by the difference in size, composition or structure of the hybridization derivatives. Therefore, the morphologies, sizes and crystal structure of the prepared CDs were studied by Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and X-ray diffraction tests. As shown in Fig. 1c and d, the TEM images presented that the CDs was uniform quasi-spherical particles with an average particle size of 3.35 nm. Similarly, Zhang et al. [12] and Xu et al. [13] also confirmed the uniform dimensions of CDs through TEM result. Fig. 1e illustrated that CDs had two peaks centered at 21.29° and 41.79°, corresponding to the (002) and (100) planes of graphite with the layer spacing of 0.34 nm and 0.21 nm, respectively. The weak peak at 41.79° is also observed in the porous C, S-porous C, N-porous C and S,N-porous C samples reported by Rong et al., referring to the (100) lattice planes of disordered carbon [6]. Moreover, the d spacing of 0.21 nm in HRTEM image of the inset of Fig. 1c matches the (100) plane of XRD pattern, suggesting that the core of the CDs had the graphitic crystal containing sp 2 nanodomains [6,23]. UV–Vis spectra were examined due to the absorption of light by π-conjugated electrons in the sp 2 atomic framework of CDs. Fig. 1f showed the broad peak at 230 nm which can be attributed to the π-π* transition of aromatic CC bonds, while the peak at 310 nm was attributed to n-π* transition of CO bonds [20]. FTIR spectrum of the product was collected to identify the nature of the functional groups of the CDs (Fig. 1g). Interestingly, CDs exhibited only two broad absorption band over 3000 cm−1 and 1649 cm−1 which is attributed to COO–H/C–OH and CO/CC [12,24], respectively, indicating a single precursor polypropylene surgical masks must undergo a complex inner heat and mass transfer chemical reaction processes in a closed vessel at high temperature and pressure, resulting in the individual functional groups. Recently, Yu ‘s research group had proposed that the inner fluid behavior remains a ‘‘black box’’ in hydrothermal reactor, which is affected by temperature difference, solution viscosity and reactor parameters [25]. Polypropylene organic carbon sources were mixed in nitric acid solution and happened a series of assembly, polymerization, cross-linking and carbonation behaviors driven by high temperature and pressure. There is an evidence linking polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbon species to soot formation [26]. It is a reasonable association that carbon clusters with disordered carbon surrounded by more ordered shells of graphite crystallite through successive coupling stabilized radicals reactions under similar chemical conditions. Previous work had shown polypropylene plastics were degraded with nitric acid to produce a mixture of dicarboxylic acids, such as malonate and adipate by a zip depolymerization reaction [27,28]. Under the hydrothermal circumstance, the various nitrogen intermediates like nitrogen oxides, nitrous acid (HNO2), nitrosonium ion (NO+), trinitramide [N4O6 2+] etc. [29], is also considered as the intermediate formed during the oxidation of plastics, which can help to cleave the hydrogen bonds in plastics and then monomers polymerized into carbon at mild conditions. In addition, it is should be noted that the O–H and C–H bonds in plastics were activated by high-pressure water, which may act as a Brønsted acid catalyst with the release of H+ and OH− with high energies, leading to the formation of C–C bonds and promoting the process of carbonization through carboxyl and alkyl radical [27,30]. The formation of the aromatic structure might be similar to that of the soot with aromatic structure through intramolecular and intermolecular rearrangement [26]. Overly harsh conditions such as the temperature reached 200 °C or prolonging the time could induce further degradation of initially formed dicarboxylic acids to gaseous products [27], leading to weak fluorescence intensity of CDs, which is also confirmed in Fig. 1b. The primary degradation mechanism could be adjusted to generate cyclic products (both alkylaromatics and alkylnaphthenes) by chain scission on the PP backbone and n-alkane aromatization for this endothermic reaction [1]. Finally, the aromatic structure may assemble to form the carbon core triggered by the annular convection during the synthetic process and favors the formation of granular particulates. And the CDs with core-shell-like structure (graphitic carbon nuclei at the core and organic functional groups such as carboxyl groups at the surface) are formed with well dispersion.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence properties in the presence of Cr (VI)
ce:section.ce:para:
To determine the emission center of the prepared CDs, excitation-dependent fluorescence spectra were tested by changing the excitation wavelength from 300 to 400 nm at intervals of 10 nm. At the optimal excitation wavelength of 340 nm, the CDs exhibited strong emission peaks at 394 and 486 nm, corresponding to the purple and cyan regions in the spectrum (Fig. 2 a), which belonged to the typical double emission fluorescent material with self-calibration function that are mainly based on the determination of the ratio of fluorescence intensities at two well-resolved emission peak [22]. This result indicates that the formation of carbon dots with dual fluorescence peaks in our study followed a different mechanism compared with carbon quantum dots with single PL emission spectrum centered at 500 nm prepared by carbonization of polyethylene [10], likely due to the different precursors with different molecule structure and synthetic condition. Meanwhile, red-shifted phenomenon from our current sample was observed, showing the CDs possessed an excitation-dependent fluorescence emission behavior due to their diverse traps in CDs [31]. Understanding the fluorescence mechanism of CDs remains a challenge and various mechanistic models have been proposed to explain their origin of luminescence [32]. The excitation-dependent behavior of carbon dots could result from an interplay of many factors that includes a combination of the carbon core, surface defects, and molecular state. It has been established that the increase in the degree of surface oxidation on CDs' surface, such as –OH, –COOH comprised the surface defects, resulting in the redshift in PL emission [32]. Since CDs samples have approximately uniform dimensions, it is reasonable consider that surface defects are a major factor in excitation-dependent PL behavior. The “molecular state” mechanism serves as the sole emission center, thus yielding excitation-independent which can be ruled out in the present study. The origin of CDs photoluminescence has been related to sp
2 domain size and the abundance of oxidized surface defects. It has been hypothesized that surface epoxides or hydroxyls can induce significant local distortion and then create new energy levels between n-π* gaps, consequently causing a wide range of excitation energies and excitation wavelength-dependent PL [33]. The present observation was also in line with the previous reported facts that increasing the degree of surface oxidation at the nanoscale surface could produce more the emissive trap population resulting in the red-shift in PL emission [34,35]. Although conjugated domains have been used to explain the fluorescence emission in CDs, it is worthy to note that they have their inherent limitation due to the simplicity of the employed model. CDs' structure is certainly more convoluted than a simple graphene structure and they are possibly comprised of several stacked layers of graphene-like together. The UV–vis absorption spectrum with broad peak at 230 nm, a shoulder at 310 nm and steady-state fluorescence spectra of CDs with strong emission peaks at 394 and 486 nm under the optimal excitation wavelength of 340 nm were presented together in Fig. 2b. CDs with dual strong emission peaks in our sample is good to directly adopt as ratiometric FL probes without introducing other optical materials by physical mixing or nanohybrid strategy [22]. The fluorescent color of the CDs was accurately shown by the Commission Internationale De L’ Eclairage (Fig. 2c) with CIE coordinates of (0.1813, 0.2627) corresponding to the blue region, which is observable by the naked eye after irradiation with 365 nm UV light (the inset of Fig. 1f). Moreover, the long-term photostability of CDs stored in water under air condition was also investigated, and the results showed that the fluorescence intensity did not obvious diminish after 30 days of storage, which was longer than that of the previous reported O-CDs (21 days) [36], indicating that our current CDs prepared from polypropylene surgical masks have good photostability (Fig. 2d).

Due to the abundant organic functional groups on the surface of CDs, they were designed as a fluorescent probe for metal ions detection. The selectivity of CDs towards Cr (VI) and other interfering ions were investigated, including Hg2+, Na+, Cu2+, NH4 +, Se2 2+, Cr3+, Cl− and Bi3+. It was observed that only the presence of Cr (VI) made fluorescence intensity to a significant decrease while the addition of other ions had little effect on the fluorescence intensity ratio from the FL spectra and optical image (Fig. 3 a and c). Specially, the fluorescence intensity of the two peaks (394 nm and 486 nm) of the ratiometric fluorescent probe in the “activated” state were simultaneously quenched in the presence of Cr (VI), which belongs to the dual-response-Off type for ratiometric probe [22,37]. The present system we studied is a neutral environment and the main chemical form of Cr (VI) is CrO4 2−. It is well known that Cr2O7 2− existed under acidic conditions [38] and the related detection are under investigations. Based on the presence of NH4 + and Cl− derived form NH4Cl in Fig. 3a, there is not a significant change in the fluorescence intensity of the CDs, indicating no obvious quenching of the CDs by Cl−. Subsequently, the practical detection of CDs in complex coexisting other ions environment, namely the competition experiment of Cr (VI) ions, was performed to evaluate the capability of a detection system. As shown in Fig. 3b (cyan striped pillars), after adding Cr (VI) ions (10 mM) into CDs water dispersion containing other ions (10 mM), the fluorescence was significantly quenched, indicating that excellent selectivity and anti-interference ability in competing multi-metal cation and anion mixtures especially chloride ions which are the main components in seawater. The result could avoid the numerous interferences generated by salt matrix and realizing the rapid and accurate determination of Cr in simulated salt water without the matrix separation steps [16]. And the effects of various Cr (VI) ions concentrations on the CDs FL were studied in detail (Fig. 3d). Under the excitation at 340 nm, the emission intensity at 394 and 486 nm decreased gradually with the increase of Cr (VI) concentration. Fig. 3e indicated the ratio I 394/I 486 and Cr (VI) ions concentration kept a linear relationship with R 2 = 0.9906 in the range from 0 to 900 μM. The regression equation can be described as:

The detection limit (LOD) for Cr (VI) ions was calculated to be 24 nM (1.2 × 10−6 mg/L) according to 3 times of standard deviations of the blank solution, which was far lower than the maximum contamination standard of 0.1 mg/L for Cr6+ in drinking water of the US Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) [38] and 0.05 mg/L (0.96 μM) permitted by World Health Organization's (WHO) guidelines [39], respectively. The comparison with previous literature reported values and other analytical methods were listed in Table S1. It is clearly seen that the LODs of the present fluorescence method (24 nM) is better than that of Colorimetry (1690 nM) and is closed to that of Voltammetry (23 nM). However, fluorescence method has the advantages of low cost, convenience and time-saving compared to that of Voltammetry and Colorimetry, which are cumbersome and need expensive instruments. Clearly, the detection value of Cr (VI) of our samples were slightly larger than that of 18 nM from carbon nanoparticles reported by Yahyazadeh et al. [40] and far lower than those values of 1.6, 1.17, 0.9 μM, in previous literatures, respectively [31–43]. Moreover, our result was much smaller than most of the CDs-based fluorescent probes such as CNF-TCDs using trimethylbenzene-stained azo dye red and urea as precursors (2.94 μM) [20], BNCDs using 3-Aminophenylboronic acid (410 nM) [21], CDs synthesized from phenylenediamine (37 nM) [23], N-CDs using maleic anhydride as precursors (120 nM) [44], Tea-CDs prepared from waste tea extract (810 nM) [45], and ACDs using sorbic acid and proline as precursors (34 nM) [46]. Although some progress has been made in the utilization of CDs-based fluorescent probes for the detection of analytes, there are still some limitations including the controlled synthesis, purification, and surface modification of CDs. At present, fluorescence quenching mechanisms mainly include fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), photoinduced electron transfer (PET) and internal filtration effect (IFE) etc., which require a certain degree of energy level match between the fluorophore and the quencher [47,48]. Specially, it was found that an obvious overlap between the UV absorption band of Cr (VI) and the excitation band of CDs in Fig. 4 a, which may cause the weaken excitation and quenching result originated from IFE effect. The calculated average fluorescence lifetimes of CDs and CDs/Cr (VI) are 3.14 ns and 3.53 ns, respectively in Fig. S1, showing the quenching mechanism of CDs on Cr (VI) is caused by the static quenching of IFE, which is agreement with the DECQDs and DECQDs/Cr (VI) (2.41 ns and 2.56 ns) [38] and N-CDs and N-CDs/Cr (VI) (3.31 ns and 3.39 ns) [41]. Interestingly, most reported detection of Cr (VI) works were attributed to the IFE mechanism [40–46]. To verify simultaneously dual FL peaks quenched process in the presence of Cr (VI), the highest occupied orbital (HOMO) and the lowest unoccupied orbital (LUMO) energy levels of CDs were investigated. The band gap (E g) of CDs was calculated using the Tauc-plot equation as shown in Eq. (2), and the band gap between HOMO and LUMO based on the absorption spectrum of carbon nuclei was about 3.49 eV (Fig. 4b), which was slightly smaller than the values of CDs (E g = 3.66 eV and 3.59 eV) [48,49].
(2)

(
h v −
E g
)
n / 2

In the formula, α, h, v represent the absorption coefficient, Planck's constant, the incident photon frequency, hv, A represent the photon energy and the proportionality constant, the index n refers to the type of semiconductor, and the value of n is generally taken to be 1 for the carbon dot type material. It can be extrapolated to establish a linear region with hv, in which hv is substituted by 1024/wavelength, and the intercept of the linear fitting line in the x-axis is E g. Therefore, in Fig. 4c, there may be an energy transfer process between the carbon core and the surface state. Obviously, the emission peak of 394 nm is probably attributed to the transition from the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) to the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) of carbon core. Similarly, the emission peak centered at 486 nm may be attributed to the transition from HOMO to the LUMO of surface state (CO related levels). Therefore, the fluorescence intensity of the two peaks (394 nm and 486 nm) of the ratiometric fluorescent probe in the “activated” state were simultaneously quenched in the presence of Cr (VI) during the IFE process, which belongs to the dual-response-Off type. Strictly speaking, the PL of CDs is not “quenched”, while it is simply “blocked” from the detector that the excited light ∼340 nm is absorbed by the Cr (VI) target analytes before it reaches the CDs detector, resulting in a decreasing of dual PL peaks intensity of the CDs.

Consequently, we could explore the luminescent characteristics of the CDs to achieve information encryption by combining the responses of signals of UV and white lights, as illustrated by the schematic diagram in Fig. 4d. For this purpose, we utilized the fluorescent ink made from the CDs to shape and illuminate the HIT pattern (HIT representing the initial letters of Harbin Institute of Technology). Fig. 4d clearly showed the optical map of the printed paper-based material under visible light and 365 nm UV light. Under white daylight, the “HIT” badges are as white as empty filter papers. The pattern showed a bright blue fluorescence under 365 nm UV excitation. Each letter and Chinese character can be clearly identified, but it is almost invisible under visible light. Specially, due to the high photostability of CDs, the pattern could be kept as natural color and exhibit a bright blue fluorescence for one year. While under the Cr (VI) solution treatments, the fluorescence of the Harbin Institute of Technology emblem pattern displayed on the paper was weak. Obviously, the optical anti-counterfeiting encryption material with Cr (VI) response recognition was developed, thus broadening the potential application of advanced image encryption and hiding specific information. These results clearly elucidated that the fabricated paper strip could be used for semi-quantitative detection of Cr (VI) in various real wastewater system observed immediately by naked eyes.

Few research is concerned about the preparation of high effective CDs inhibitor in neutral salt solution, and its adsorption and anticorrosion mechanism hasn't been made clear. Therefore, the inhibition performance of CDs was investigated systematically on copper in neutral 3.5 wt% NaCl solution by various electrochemical techniques.

The variations in open circuit potential (OCP) with time are a key indicator for assessing corrosion inhibitors performance. Fig. 5 a showed the OCP plots of a copper immersed in 3.5 wt% NaCl medium with various CDs concentrations (25–200 mg/L). It is noteworthy that the addition of CDs resulted in a considerable negative shift in open circuit potential, less than 85 mV compared to the blank solution, indicating that CDs belong to mixed corrosion inhibitor [12]. Differently, Xu et al. reported the OCP of biomass carbon dots possessed exceeding 85 mV when compared to the blank solution [13], which may be attributed to different corrosion inhibitor including physical or chemical process for different surface state of CDs. Fig. 5a showed that OCP in solutions containing CDs exhibited a decreasing trend at the initial 120 s due to the dissolution of oxides on copper [13,50]. And then, the OCP plots eventually stabilized at approximately 400 s, suggesting the successful the formation of a protective film inhibiting further oxidation and corrosion of the copper surface.

Potentiodynamic polarization (PDP) technique is applied to further investigate the process and mechanism of the anodic and cathodic reactions in order to evaluate the inhibitory effect of CDs on these reactions. Fig. 5b depicted PDP curves of copper electrode in various test concentration solutions, and classical extrapolation was used to calculate the Tafel part of PDP curves [13], where the corrosion current density values are estimated accurately by extrapolating the cathodic and anodic linear region. The correlation parameters were summarized in Table S2 including corrosion potential (E corr), corrosion current density (i corr), cathode slope (β c), anode slope (β a) and inhibition efficiency (η). Among them, η value is recorded as the following formula:

× 100 %
where i corr,0 and i corr are corrosion current densities in the absence and presence of the inhibitor, respectively. Combined with Fig. 5b and Table S2, the corrosion current densities of blank copper gradually rises to a higher value, which is due to the oxidation of Cu to Cu+ formation under the attack of Cl−. Both cathodic and anodic regions shifted to lower i corr (781.4 μA/cm2 for 0 mg/L CDs and 66.1 μA/cm2 for 200 mg/L CDs) with CDs concentrations increasing, indicating that CDs had an overt inhibiting effect and reduced attack of Cl− on the Cu surface on both cathodic and anodic reactions as mixed type inhibitor due to formation of CDs barrier film [12,51,52]. Correspondingly, η values reflecting the inhibition capacity dramatically increased from 84.11 % for 25 mg/L CDs to 90.12 % for 100 mg/L CDs, and achieved maximum of 91.54 % in 200 mg/L CDs solution. Notably, the solution containing 200 mg/L CDs showed a plateau of −0.35 V in the anodic branch compared to the blank solution (−0.27 V), while the negative shift is due to selective inhibition of oxygen reduction in cathodic reaction and change in adsorption behavior with the increase in concentration of CDs [12,13,52]. In comparison, all of the cathodic branches of the polarization curves are parallel with each other, which implies that CDs are treated as mixed type inhibitor with greater extent of anodic suppression than the cathodic one [52].

EIS is a promising technique for investigating the adsorption of corrosion inhibitors on metal surfaces through evaluating electrochemical behavior and the effectiveness of corrosion inhibitors [13]. Fig. 5 c, d displayed the Nyquist and Bode plots for a copper electrode treated by 3.5 wt% NaCl medium at different concentrations of CDs. As the concentration of CDs increases, the capacitive response induced by the dense protective film adsorbed on the electrode surface effectively retarded the charge transfer process, and the loop diameter in the high-frequency region increased remarkably [13,51]. It is worth mentioning that these capacitive loops were not perfect semicircles due to frequency dispersion effects caused by roughness and inhomogeneity of the electrode surface [53]. According to the Bode data as shown in Fig. 5d, the impedance modulus at the lowest frequency (0.01 Hz) (i.e., |Z|0.01 Hz) can be evaluated for the corrosion rate of the copper electrode. The corrosion rate tends to decrease with increasing values of |Z|0.01 Hz [51,53,54]. CDs-200 mg/L possessed the highest |Z|0.01 Hz value of 610 Ω•cm2, boosting 83.7 % than the |Z|0.01 Hz of the blank group (100 Ω•cm2). Our value is slight bigger than that of fructose and saccharose derived FCDs-0.5 (593.5 Ω•cm2) and SCDs-0.5 (443.2 Ω•cm2) reported by He et al. [51] and that of citric acid-based CDs (432.4 Ω•cm2) [54], and smaller than that of 6962.3 Ω•cm2 with the addition of 200 mg/L melamine modified CDs for Q235 carbon steel [53]. Moreover, the phase angle became higher in the presence of CDs, indicating the adsorption of the CDs inhibitor in the copper/solution interface and a protective film formed [51].

To better understand the corrosion process, Table S3 listed the electrochemical parameter values of the corresponding fitted results from EIS data. Among electrochemical parameter values, R s, R f and R ct stand for the resistance of the electrolyte and corrosion product layer and charge transfer resistance, respectively. Here, the R f of all samples increased with higher corrosion inhibitor concentration as shown in Fig. S2, proving the formation of adsorbed films and inhibition of interfacial corrosion [13]. Furthermore, the suppressed efficiency (η) of CDs on copper in 3.5 wt% NaCl medium is evaluated by the following formula (4) from the R ct:
(4)
η
( % )
=
R ct
−
R
ct , 0
R ct
× 100 %
where the R ct and R ct,0 represent the charge transfer resistance with and without corrosion inhibitors respectively. In Table S3, the R ct values revealed a stark rising and even boosted more than 16 times (from 66.73 to 1114 Ω•cm2) with the addition of 200 mg/L CDs, which was similar to GCDs-25 with 17 times (from 11.5 to over 200 Ω•cm2) with glucose as a precursor [51]. In addition, constant phase components CPE f denotes capacitance of the deposited film, CPE dl expresses the double-layer capacitor. The modulus of CPE can be computed using Eqs. (5) and (6), where j is the imaginary root, f max is the frequency at maximum imaginary part, Y 0 is the amplitude of the CPE, and N is the dispersion index with a phase shift range of −1 to 1, owing to the capacitive behavior formed by the nanoparticles anchored to the copper surface, the Y 0 fitted value becomes smaller with the addition of CDs [12].

The data (Nyquist plots and Bode plots) obtained above can be fitted with suitable equivalent circuit diagrams as shown in Fig. 5e and f, so as to quantitatively analyze the adsorption behavior of corrosion inhibitor on the metal surface. Besides CDs concentration effect, the influence of different immersion time on the corrosion inhibition efficiency of CDs was also investigated. There was a large negative shift in the open circuit potential with increasing adsorption time of CDs on the copper in Fig. S3a. Additionally, the desorption behavior of the anode in Fig. S3b was significantly positively displaced with time, and the corrosion current density was considerably reduced, leading to a much improved corrosion inhibition performance [12]. Furthermore, it can be seen from Fig. S3c that the arc radius of the resistive increased with the immersion time (from 310.8 Ω•cm2 to more than 931.2 Ω•cm2), which might be owing to the increase in interfacial impedance, thus exerting a good protective function on the Cu surface. In the corresponding Bode diagram (Fig. S3d), the CPE f was obviously smaller (from 2.15 × 10−3 F•cm−2 to 1.14 × 10−4 F•cm−2) which is superior to N-CDs (from 9.46 × 10−6 F•cm−2 to 5.85 × 10−6 F•cm−2) [12], showing that the capacitive behavior was more pronounced and the thickness of the layer more concentrated and stable. The value of impedance modulus of copper was increased considerably after prolonged immersion compared to short time. This proved that the corrosion inhibition capacity of CDs is time dependent and stabilized at 24 h [12]. Based on the fitted parameters listed in Table S4, the protective ability η of CDs against copper after 24 h of adsorption was as high as 92.83 % compared with the blank solution, indicating CDs exhibits excellent inhibition performance for copper in 3.5 wt% NaCl medium.

In order to further understand the adsorption of mask-derived CDs on the copper surface, a variety of adsorption isotherm models, including Flory-Huggins, El-Awady, Freundlich, Temkin, and Langmuir were used [52]. By comparing the fitting results of various adsorption isotherms (Fig. S4) and the corresponding linear regression coefficient (R2), as shown in Table S5, Langmuir isotherm with R2 = 0.99996 was more suitable for studying the interaction between mask-CDs and copper surface (Fig. 6 a). The corresponding adsorption isotherms are acquired by the following equation:
(7)
θ
1 − θ
=
K ads
C
where θ, K ads and C are the surface coverage, adsorption equilibrium constant and inhibitor concentration respectively. The functions of C/θ versus C were displayed in Fig. 6a, we can conclude that K ads = 0.38333 L/mg, which is slightly greater than glucose-based functionalized CDs (FCDs) at 6 h immersion of copper (0.33684 L/mg) [14]. Moreover, the calculated values of K ads and the adsorption free energy (ΔG ads) were presented in the diagrams, which are deduced by the following equation [14]:
(8)
Δ
G ads
= − R T
ln
(
1000
K ads
)
where R and T are the molar gas constant and absolute temperature (298 K) respectively. The magnitude of the ΔG ads value can be employed to determine the type of adsorption. In general, ΔG ads values below −40 kJ/mol are classified as chemisorption which can be adhered to the metal surface due to the presence of CO functional groups and above −20 kJ/mol as physisorption due to the electrostatic interaction between the charged metal and organic compounds [12,14,52]. Clearly, the calculated ΔG ads in our present work is −36.45 kJ mol−1, which is slightly greater than citric acid and l-serine CDs (−27.25 kJ mol−1) [12], glucose-based functionalized carbon dots (FCDs) (−31.55 kJ mol−1) [14], and is smaller than an imidazopyrimidine dye corrosion inhibitor (−38.47 kJ mol−1) [52]. Obviously, our result indicated the CDs could be firmly interaction between the inhibitors and copper surface involving mixed mode of spontaneous adsorption (both physisorption and chemisorption), which is well agreement with previous report [52].

Weight loss tests were carried out to investigate the corrosion inhibition properties of in situ prepared CDs for copper in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution [51]. The corrosion rates of copper samples and inhibition efficiency (η) values of various concentrations of CDs after soaking for 24 h in various groups at 25 °C are presented in Fig. 6b. After adding the inhibitor of 200 mg/L to the corrosion environment of 3.5 wt% NaCl solution, the corrosion rate of copper rate was significantly reduced to 1.02 × 10−3 g•cm−2•h−1. Particularly, the η values for all CDs exceeded 80 % and CDs-200 mg/L possessed the highest η value of 94.01 %, followed by CDs-100 mg/L (89.82 %) and CDs-50 mg/L (87.14 %). Similarly, the corrosion rate and inhibition efficiency (η) values of copper specimens at CDs concentration of 200 mg/L were investigated for different immersion times. Fig. 6c showed the corrosion rate of copper samples decreased dramatically from 6.4 × 10−2 g•cm−2•h−1 to 1.8 × 10−3 g•cm−2•h−1, which is far lower than FCDs-0.5 (0.12 g•cm−2•h−1) reported by He et al. [51]. Combining the above results, CDs-200 mg/L has the highest corrosion inhibition efficiency of 94.01 % at an immersion time of 24 h (Fig. 6d), which is higher than the values of most previous literatures [13,14,53,54]. For instance, the corrosion inhibition efficiency of glucose-based CDs (FCDs) [14] and biomass CDs (BCDs) [13] for copper were 88 % and 92.97 %, respectively. The corrosion inhibition efficiency of melamine modified CDs (Me-CDs) [53] and imidazole ionic liquids and citric acid-based CDs (IM-CDs) [54] for Q235 steel were 92.36 % and 85.7 %, respectively. In addition, the corrosion inhibition efficiency was slightly lower than that of N-CDs from melamine and fructose with 96.1 % for copper in 3 wt% NaCl solution [55]. Details (substrate, corrosive medium, concentration of inhibitors, and inhibition efficiency) involved was in Fig. 6d and Table S6.

XPS tests were used to acquire information on the composition of the molecular protective film, the oxidation state and bonding condition between copper and the CDs immersed in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution. In Fig. 7 a, peaks of carbon, oxygen and copper appeared in the full XPS spectrum, indicating that the CDs had been adsorbed onto the copper surface. Two C 1s spectra peaks can be visualized at 284.8 and 288.64 eV in Fig. 7b, which was agreement with previous reported C–C/CC peak and C–O/CO peak [13]. The O 1s spectrum in Fig. 7c had three peaks ascribed to CuO/Cu2O, CO, and C–OH at 531.03, 531.76, and 532.50 eV, respectively [13]. The Cu 2p1/2/Cu (I) and Cu 2p3/2/Cu (I) XPS peaks in Fig. 7d were observed at 952.08 eV and 932.35 eV [13,56]. In the meantime, a small amount of Cu (II) with weak peaks at 934.78 eV and 955.68 eV corresponding to Cu (II) 2p3/2 and Cu (II) 2p1/2, were still present. The appearance of the Cu (I) peak demonstrated that the adding of CDs reduced attack of Cl− on the Cu surface and prevented CuCl from transformation into soluble [CuCl2]- (cuprous chloride) complex and Cu2+ [52]. The produced specimens were further characterized by FTIR after being soaked in 3.5 wt% NaCl medium containing 200 mg/L CDs at 298 K for 24 h to probe the potential mechanism. Fig. 7f revealed that the broad range of 3600-3000 cm−1 was due to the presence of COO–H/O–H on the CDs surface [12]. It is apparent that the shift of the CO (1649 cm−1) peak is related to the formation of chelating bonds between O groups and Cu (I) and the weak peak at 624.34 cm−1 is probably connected to Cu2O bond [13]. The Raman spectrum of the prepared CDs modified Cu specimens was displayed in Fig. 7g. The peaks in the ranges of 600–720 cm−1 and 2950-3100 cm−1 were assigned to the vibrations of the CDs aromatic ring and the C–H bond, respectively. The strong peaks between 1900 and 2400 cm−1 were ascribed from the vibrational absorption bones of sp
3 and sp
2 carbon atoms in graphene [12], and the peak of Cu–O appeared around 227 cm−1, which was similar to the previous report of Cu2O sample (211 cm−1) [57], further reflecting the interactions between the CDs and Cu samples. After the corrosion test, the corrosion surface patterns of copper in different solutions were shown in Fig. S5. Clearly, the bare and clean copper surface was heavily corroded by Cl− and O2 of saltine water and covered with a layer of loose corrosion Cu-based products. In contrast, the addition of in-situ prepared mask-derived CDs corrosion inhibitor resulted in a significant reduction for copper corrosion, which was also consistent with electrochemical results. Moreover, the reversible on-off fluorescence of the CDs under the UV light irradiation further confirmed the interaction between CDs film and copper surface (Fig. S5).

Based on the above-mentioned analyses of electrochemistry and corrosive surfaces, a effective protection model in neutral saltine environment was reasonably proposed to illustrate the inhibition mechanism of the in situ prepared CDs (Fig. 8 ). According to literature, the electrochemical reaction equation for copper in neutral sodium chloride solution is [14,52]:
(9) Cathode: O2+2H2O+4e−→4OH−
(10) Anode: 4Cu + O2→2Cu2O
(11) Cu + Cl−→CuClads + e−
(12) CuClads + Cl−→CuCl2 −

Meanwhile, the hydrolysis of CuCl2 − is expressed as follows:
(13) 2CuCl2 − + H2O→Cu2O+4Cl-+2H+

Reaction (12) is the rate-determining step in which the diffusion of soluble copper species or dissolved oxygen transport is the main influencing factor. The anodic reaction also involves the process in which the copper substrate loses one electron and converts it to Cu+ with the valence electron configuration ([Ar] 3 d10). The following step primarily involves the transformation of Cu + to Cu2+, which requires higher ionization energy to overcome [13], so there was only a small amount of Cu2+ which is consistent with the XPS results. Therefore, based on the chemical corrosive equations, surface functional groups of CDs, O2 and Cl− played a vital role. Firstly, CDs laid on the Cu metal surface via electrostatic attraction force of physical adsorption [51]. Secondly, the CDs possessing abundant oxygen-containing functional groups easily formed coordination bonds between the lone pair electrons of O atoms and the unoccupied 3d orbitals of Cu atoms (chemical adsorption), which can be confirmed by the present XPS and FTIR results. Similarly, it is reported that the formate molecules can form an ultrathin surface coordination layer protected by copper formate dimer [Cu(μ-HCOO) (OH)2]2 paddle-wheel units, which effectively prevents O2 from interacting with the internal metallic copper atoms [58]. Therefore, it is reasonable that the carboxyl groups on the surface of our prepared CDs may also have this effect. In addition, further understanding the behavior of CDs in water requires the consideration of a interfacial dielectric surrounding between Cu and CDs. CDs had been reported to own unique oxygen-harvesting properties and can reduce the oxygen content of near the copper surface to create a pronounced concentration gradient, thus blocking the cathodic oxygen reduction reaction, which may also be a key factor to act as corrosion inhibitors [10,59]. Besides oxygen, CDs exhibited the existing chloride binding mechanism as well as the fact of CNTs and GO with chloride binding properties and CDs was firstly applied to anti-chloride-corrosion ability by enhancing chloride binding performance of cement [60]. Based on the above discussion, in summary, it can be accepted that the greater number of oxygen-containing functional groups confers more prominent corrosion inhibition properties to CDs, which may contribute to the formation of a more compact and thicker protective film. Moreover, the oxygen and chloride binding mechanism of CDs should be further deeply investigated in the future and the related experiments are in progress.

In summary, dual-emitting water-soluble CDs were successfully synthesized via a hydrothermal conversion method using face mask single precursor. The selective response to Cr (VI) ions indicated that the prepared CDs had the potential to be a novel dual-response-Off type ratiometric probe with detection limit of 24 nM, which was better than most of the reported literature. In addition, the in situ prepared CDs corrosion inhibitors for copper in 3.5 wt% NaCl medium demonstrated the corrosion inhibition efficiency of 94.01 % under a CDs concentration of 200 mg/L at room temperature. The Cr (VI) detection and Cu inhibition mechanism of CDs were ascribed to dual-response-OFF type induced by inner filter effect (IFE) mechanism and the formation of CDs-adsorbed protective film, respectively. This study may pave new possibilities for the sustainable production of carbon materials with bifunction of fluorescence metal monitoring and corrosion protection in neutral high salinity solutions and high-value-added utilization of “white-pollution” disposable polymer wastes.
